source: iConnections Global Alts 2026 — AI, Tesla, defense, energy — what comes next
summary: Space-based compute (racks in space connected by laser) is a viable and strategically important architecture
summary_extended: Baker outlines a specific technical vision for space-based compute in which scaled Starlink V3 satellites — each generating approximately 100kW of power — are connected by laser inter-satellite links to form a distributed orbital data center. He argues that lasers operating in vacuum propagate faster than signals through terrestrial fiber, enabling lower latency than any ground-based cloud architecture, and that this latency advantage could command pricing premiums analogous to (or exceeding) the ~70% premium cloud providers already charge for proximity-based compute. Baker frames this architecture as strategically critical for US competitiveness, suggesting sovereign and defense use cases beyond commercial cloud. The investment implication is long-term and speculative: if orbital compute becomes viable, SpaceX (via Starlink) would be the primary infrastructure provider, but SpaceX remains private, leaving no clean US-listed pure-play equity expression of this thesis at present.
detail: Baker outlines a specific technical vision: scaled Starlink V3 satellites (~100kW each) connected by laser links forming a distributed data center in orbit. He notes lasers in vacuum are faster than fiber, enabling lower latency than terrestrial cloud. Cloud providers already earn a 70% premium for proximity; space-based compute could command similar or greater premiums. He frames this as important for US competitiveness.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents specific technical specs (20kW Starlink V3, 130kW Blackwell rack) suggesting deep research, but this is a forward-looking architectural thesis
quote_fragment: racks in space connected by lasers that's your data center
